cards.text:
Do-while loop syntax:
{{c1::do}}
{
    // statements
}
{{c2::while (condition);}}
Note: semicolon required after condition!

cards.front: What is the key difference between while and do-while loops?
cards.back: Do-while checks the condition AFTER each iteration, so the loop body always executes at least once. While checks BEFORE, so the body may never execute.

cards.front: How many times is a do-while loop guaranteed to execute?
cards.back: At least once, because the condition is checked after the first iteration.

cards.question: What does this code print?
cards.explanation: Even though x < 5 is false from the start, the do-while executes once before checking the condition. It prints 5, increments x to 6, then exits.

cards.front: Why does the do-while loop require a semicolon after the condition?
cards.back: The while(condition); is actually a complete statement in a do-while loop, so it needs a semicolon like any other statement.

cards.text: Unlike regular while loops, do-while loops require a {{c1::semicolon}} after the while condition.

cards.concept: When to use do-while vs while
cards.explanation:
Use do-while when:
  • You need the loop body to execute at least once
  • Common for menu systems or input validation
  • You want to prompt user before checking input
Use while when:
  • The loop might need to execute zero times
  • This is the more common case (95%+ of loops)
  • More intuitive to read (condition at top)
examples:
// Good use of do-while: menu
do {
    showMenu();
    choice = getInput();
} while (choice != 0);

// Better as while: may skip entirely
while (count < 10) { count++; }

cards.front: Which is more commonly used in practice: while or do-while?
cards.back: While loops are far more common. Do-while is rarely used because most loops need to check the condition before executing.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Missing semicolon after while(x < 5). Do-while requires a semicolon after the condition.
cards.explanation: Unlike regular while loops, do-while statements require a semicolon after the condition because while(condition); is a complete statement.

cards.front: What is the most common use case for do-while loops?
cards.back: Input validation and menu systems where you want to prompt the user at least once before checking their response.

cards.question: Why is do-while good for this scenario?
cards.answer: We need to prompt the user at least once before we can validate their input.
cards.explanation: A do-while guarantees one prompt before checking the condition. With a regular while, we'd need to initialize selection or duplicate the prompt code.
